source_label: Satoshi Nakamoto — Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
excerpt: Transactions broadcast on a best-effort basis. Nodes do not need to forward every transaction; they just need to forward enough that miners can build blocks. The mempool is the natural staging area for this.
body:
Source excerpt:
Transactions broadcast on a best-effort basis. Nodes do not need to forward every transaction; they just need to forward enough that miners can build blocks. The mempool is the natural staging area for this.

---

Cited by leaf "The mempool, fee estimation, and RBF":
The *mempool* is a node's local cache of unconfirmed transactions it has received and is willing to relay. It is *per-node*, not global — every node's mempool is slightly different, depending on which transactions reached it, in what order, and which policy filters it runs. The mempool is consulted by miners when they assemble candidate blocks, and by wallets when they estimate fees. It is not part of consensus [1].

The distinction between *consensus rules* and *policy* matters here. Consensus rules are the laws every full node enforces: a block is valid or it isn't. Policy is the local convention for which transactions a node is *willing to relay and mine* — non-standard transactions might be perfectly valid in a block, but Core nodes refuse to gossip them onto the network or include them in templates. The mempool is the place where this distinction lives. A non-standard transaction that arrives in a mined block is accepted; the same transaction arriving via P2P gossip is dropped silently [2].

*Replace-by-fee* (RBF) is the mempool policy that allows a transaction to be replaced by a newer transaction that conflicts with it (spends at least one of the same inputs) and pays a higher fee. BIP-125 specifies the original opt-in form: a transaction signals its replaceability by setting a sequence number below `0xfffffffe`. Full RBF, which makes every transaction implicitly replaceable, is a more recent and contested policy change [2]. RBF is what allows users to bump a stuck transaction's fee instead of waiting hours for it to confirm or expire.

*Child-pays-for-parent* (CPFP) is the complementary mechanism: a recipient who finds an incoming transaction stuck can spend its output in a new high-fee transaction, and miners — who select transactions by *package* fee rate, not individual fee rate — will include the parent to claim the child's fee. This lets a recipient unstick a transaction the sender forgot about.

Fee estimation is empirical. Bitcoin Core's `estimatesmartfee` walks the recent confirmation history (how full were recent blocks, how quickly did transactions in fee bucket X confirm) to estimate what fee rate clears within N blocks. Better estimators exist as third-party services, but the Core estimator is conservative-by-design — it errs toward overpaying because the cost of being stuck is much worse than the cost of overpaying by a few sats per vbyte.

---

Cited by leaf "Peer discovery, addrman, and eclipse defenses":
Bitcoin's P2P network is unstructured: every node maintains a small set of outbound connections (8 by default in Core) and accepts up to 125 inbound connections. There is no DHT, no central directory, no routing protocol — peers gossip blocks and transactions on a best-effort basis, and the network's resilience comes from sheer redundancy [1].

A fresh node bootstraps by querying *DNS seeds*: a set of hard-coded DNS hostnames (`seed.bitcoin.sipa.be`, `dnsseed.bluematt.me`, several others) that resolve to the IPs of long-running nodes. A node might also use the hard-coded fallback list compiled into the source if DNS is unreachable. After the first connection, the new node asks its peer for additional addresses via `addr` and `getaddr` messages, populates its address manager, and stops relying on the seeds [2].

The *address manager* (addrman) is the persistent in-memory database of addresses the node has heard about. Addresses are bucketed by source-IP group and by which peer told us about them, with separate tables for 'tried' (we successfully connected) and 'new' (we haven't yet). This bucketing is the primary defense against an attacker poisoning addrman with addresses they control: even an attacker connecting from many IPs can fill at most a small fraction of the buckets, so the next outbound connection has a high probability of being a real, independent peer [2].

An *eclipse attack* is when an attacker controls all of a victim node's connections — both inbound and outbound — and can therefore control what the victim sees of the chain. A successfully-eclipsed merchant could be shown a fake block confirming a fake payment. The defenses are layered: addrman's bucketing makes it hard to monopolize the new node's outbound list, the diversity of outbound connection types (address-net groups, Tor, I2P), feeler connections that probe random addresses to refresh the table, anchor connections preserved across restarts, and asmap-based AS-aware bucketing introduced in 0.20 [3].

*Ban scoring* is the per-peer reputation system: peers that send invalid blocks, repeated invalid transactions, or other protocol violations accumulate a misbehavior score and are eventually disconnected and temporarily banned. This is not a defense against malicious peers in the cryptographic sense — bans are easy to evade by reconnecting from a new IP — but it filters honest-but-buggy peers and rate-limits trivial DoS attempts.

---

Cited by leaf "UTXO model versus account model":
Bitcoin tracks money as a set of *unspent transaction outputs* (UTXOs), not as account balances. Every transaction takes one or more existing UTXOs as inputs, destroys them, and creates one or more new UTXOs as outputs. There is no global ledger of 'who owns what' — there is only the chainstate database, which is the set of UTXOs that currently exist. A wallet's 'balance' is just the sum of the UTXOs it can spend [1].

This is the most important architectural choice in the protocol after proof of work, and it has consequences in every direction. *Validation* parallelises naturally: two transactions that don't touch the same UTXOs can be validated independently, with no shared state to lock around. *Privacy* improves because there is no canonical 'account' identity attached to a UTXO — addresses are reused only by convention, and well-behaved wallets generate a fresh address per receipt [2]. *Replay protection* is automatic: once a UTXO is spent, the same transaction cannot be replayed because its inputs no longer exist.

The alternative — the account model used by Ethereum and most legacy banking systems — keeps a balance per address. Transactions debit one balance and credit another. This is intuitively familiar and makes some smart-contract patterns easier to express. It also serialises validation (every transaction touches the global account state), creates implicit replay risk (the same nonce-less transaction could be re-broadcast), and produces stronger linkability (every transaction from an address contributes to a single canonical history) [3].

In the chainstate, each UTXO is keyed by `(txid, output_index)` and stores the `scriptPubKey` (the cryptographic puzzle that locks it) and the value in satoshis. The chainstate is what every full node maintains as its authoritative view of the current state of the world; the historical block data is interesting but not necessary for validating new blocks beyond the chainstate. This is what makes pruning possible: a node can discard old blocks once their effects on the chainstate have been applied, and continue validating new blocks indefinitely with a few gigabytes of working state.
